evals.prompt: Client received CP2000 dated March 15, 2025, proposing $14,200 of additional tax on a 2023 individual return based on $42,000 of unreported income from a Form 1099-K. The taxpayer contends the $42,000 is the GROSS payments through Stripe; the NET (after refunds and chargebacks) was $36,500, which was reported on Schedule C as $36,500. Today is March 30, 2025.
evals.expected_output: Identifies CP2000 with 30-day deadline (April 14, 2025); 15 days remain. Strategy: disagree with substantiation. Draft response showing (1) Form 1099-K reports gross payments per §6050W; (2) Schedule C properly reports net of refunds and chargebacks (or gross with refunds as a deduction line); (3) provide Stripe annual statement, refund-and-chargeback log, and reconciliation tying to Schedule C Line 1 (or Line 2 for refunds-from-receipts). Cite §6050W (third-party-payment-network reporting), §61 (gross income), and §62 / §162 / §63 framework as relevant. Recommend response by certified mail with return receipt; calendar a 45-day follow-up if no IRS response. NOT a stat notice; if IRS proceeds with adjustment, a Letter 3219 will follow with 90-day Tax Court rights.
expectations: Notes CP2000 NOT a stat notice; CP3219A or Letter 3219 may follow

evals.prompt: Client received Letter 3219 (Statutory Notice of Deficiency) dated February 1, 2025, proposing $85,000 of tax + $17,000 penalty for 2022. The taxpayer disputes the substantive adjustments. Today is April 20, 2025. Client missed the 90-day window because the notice was lost in office mail; only just discovered.
evals.expected_output: Identifies Letter 3219 with 90-day Tax Court petition deadline (May 2, 2025; 90 days from February 1) — 12 days remain. JURISDICTIONAL. PRIORITY action: prepare and file Tax Court petition immediately, even on a protective basis with claim that the substantive merits will be developed in proceedings. Filing in Tax Court suspends collection. Note: §6213(a) deadline does NOT toll for ongoing administrative discussions; missed deadline is fatal under Hallmark Research Collective, 159 T.C. No. 6 (2022). Recommend Tax Court counsel engagement for petition drafting (Tax Court Rule 34); the firm can file pro se but a tax-controversy practitioner should review. Calendar deadline for petition filing.
expectations: Cites Hallmark Research Collective on jurisdictionality
Notes filing in Tax Court suspends collection

evals.prompt: Client received CP14 dated April 18, 2025, showing a balance due of $8,400 for 2024 individual tax. The client has been compliant for the prior 3 years (timely returns and payments). She cannot pay the full $8,400 today but can pay $400/month. Today is April 25, 2025.
evals.expected_output: Identifies CP14 with 21-day pay-or-contact window. Two-step strategy: (1) FTA screen — if any §6651 family penalty was assessed (FTF or FTP), file abatement request under IRM 20.1.1.3.6 (eligibility likely met given 3-year compliance). FTA on FTP can save up to ~$420 (assume 5 months × 0.5% × $8,400). (2) Installment agreement — taxpayer qualifies for streamlined installment agreement (under $50K total liability); apply via Form 9465 or via online payment agreement (OPA) tool. $400 / month over 21 months covers principal; interest under §6601 continues to accrue at the federal short-term rate + 3%. The CP14 is a collection notice, NOT a CDP notice — CDP rights attach later if levy is threatened (Letter 1058 / CP90).
expectations: Notes interest continues to accrue under §6601
